world-news.title: Trump warns 'clock is ticking' for Iran as peace progress stalls
world-news.summary: President Donald Trump is threatening Iran while peace negotiations between the US and Iran have hit a standstill. Iran, a Middle Eastern nation with a history of tension with the US, recently submitted proposals to resolve the conflict, but the US hasn't made meaningful offers in return. Trump's ultimatum suggests time is running out for a diplomatic solution. This matters because the US-Iran conflict affects global oil prices, regional stability in the Middle East, and could potentially escalate into military action. Understanding this helps explain why gas prices and international tensions fluctuate, and why the Middle East remains a geopolitical hotspot.

world-news.title: 'Won't be anything left': Trump issues threat to Iran amid stalled talks
world-news.summary: Trump has issued a stark warning to Iran, demanding the country dismantle its nuclear program and missile stockpiles as conditions for ending the conflict. Iran's nuclear program refers to its ability to enrich uranium, which can be used for both energy and weapons—a major global security concern. Trump's hardline stance signals the US won't compromise on these demands, escalating tensions significantly. This is crucial because Iran developing nuclear weapons could trigger regional arms races, destabilize the Middle East, and draw other major powers like Russia and China into the conflict. The threat of military action looms if diplomacy fails, making this a pivotal moment in international relations.

world-news.title: WHO declares Ebola outbreak in DR Congo an international emergency
world-news.summary: The World Health Organization (WHO) has declared an Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo a global health emergency. Ebola is a rare but extremely deadly virus that spreads through contact with infected blood or body fluids, causing severe illness and death in about 50% of cases. The current outbreak has around 246 cases and 80 deaths, though it hasn't yet reached pandemic status (a pandemic spreads across multiple countries). This declaration signals the outbreak requires worldwide coordination to prevent global spread, similar to how COVID-19 became a pandemic. It affects teenagers because pandemics disrupt schools, travel, and normal life, and understanding disease outbreaks helps you recognize when to take precautions and trust public health guidance.

world-news.title: Large-scale Ukrainian drone attack kills three in Moscow region, says Russia
world-news.summary: Ukraine has conducted a significant drone attack on the Moscow region, killing three people, which Ukraine's president calls a justified response to Russian attacks on Ukrainian cities. This ongoing conflict began when Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022, and drone warfare has become a major tactic for both sides. Ukraine argues it's fighting back against Russian aggression, while Russia claims this proves Ukraine's aggression. This matters globally because the Ukraine-Russia war affects food and energy prices worldwide, threatens European security, and represents the largest military conflict in Europe since World War II. Your daily life is affected through higher gas prices, inflation, and global instability that impacts international relationships and trade.

world-news.title: UAE reports strike near Abu Dhabi nuclear power plant
world-news.summary: The United Arab Emirates reported a strike near Abu Dhabi's nuclear power plant and called it a dangerous escalation, though the country is still investigating who was responsible. A nuclear power plant is a facility that generates electricity using nuclear reactions, and attacks on such facilities pose massive risks including radiation leaks that could kill thousands and contaminate large areas. This incident, likely connected to the wider Iran-Saudi Arabia-UAE regional tensions, shows how the Middle East conflict is expanding geographically. It matters because nuclear accidents spread far beyond borders—radiation from the Chernobyl disaster in 1986 still affects Europe today. This also signals that regional conflicts are becoming more unpredictable and dangerous, potentially drawing more nations into confrontation.

world-news.title: 'This may be the last time you hear my voice': Political executions surge in Iran since start of war
world-news.summary: Since military tensions escalated between the US and Israel against Iran on February 28, the United Nations has verified that at least 32 political prisoners have been executed in Iran. Political prisoners are people arrested for opposing their government or speaking out against it, not for traditional crimes. These executions represent a dramatic increase in Iran's crackdown on dissidents and suggest the government is tightening control amid the conflict. This matters because executions of political opponents indicate authoritarian practices and human rights violations, which the international community condemns. Understanding this helps you see how wars often lead governments to suppress freedoms at home, and why human rights organizations monitor these abuses even in countries far from your own.

world-news.title: Israel kills at least five in Lebanon after 'ceasefire' extended
world-news.summary: Israel has killed at least five people in Lebanon through air attacks, even as a ceasefire agreement between Israel and Hezbollah (a militant group based in Lebanon) was supposed to be extended. A ceasefire is an agreement to stop fighting, but this one appears fragile and violated. Lebanon is already devastated by economic collapse and political dysfunction, and Israeli attacks intensify civilian suffering. This matters because the Israel-Hezbollah conflict threatens to explode into a full regional war that could drag in Iran, the US, and other powers, destabilizing the entire Middle East. For teenagers globally, this represents how fragile peace agreements can be and how regional conflicts have ripple effects on international politics, refugee crises, and global security.

world-news.title: Pakistan interior minister holds talks with Iranian president in Tehran
world-news.summary: Pakistan's Interior Minister Mohsin Naqvi met with Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian in Tehran to hold diplomatic talks. Pakistan is a major South Asian nation with historical ties to both Iran and the US, and these high-level talks suggest Pakistan is attempting to mediate or coordinate on regional issues amid the escalating US-Iran tensions. When countries send top officials for talks, it signals serious diplomatic engagement on important matters. This matters because Pakistan's position is strategically crucial—it borders Iran and Afghanistan and hosts millions of Afghan refugees, making it a key player in Middle Eastern and South Asian stability. Pakistan's diplomatic moves influence whether conflicts escalate or cool down, affecting regional peace and global geopolitical balance.

world-news.title: Will the latest Ebola outbreak in DR Congo and Uganda spread further?
world-news.summary: The World Health Organization has declared the Ebola epidemic in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda a global health emergency, raising urgent questions about whether it will spread beyond these countries. Uganda and the DRC share a border, and the virus spreading between these nations demonstrates how diseases cross borders easily. Ebola's 50% fatality rate makes it one of the deadliest viruses known, but it spreads more slowly than COVID-19, giving health organizations a better chance to contain it through isolation and medical care. This matters because global disease outbreaks remind us we're interconnected—an outbreak in Africa affects health security worldwide, and poor countries often lack resources to contain diseases effectively. Your generation will inherit these global health challenges and must understand how pandemics work to protect yourselves and others.

world-news.title: Peruvian authorities confirm Fujimori and Sanchez advance to runoff
world-news.summary: Peru's election authorities confirmed that two candidates—Fujimori and Sanchez—will face each other in a June 7 runoff election after the first round of voting was marred by logistical errors and fraud allegations. A runoff is when no candidate wins a majority in the first election, so the top two compete again. Peru is Latin America's second-largest economy and a major producer of copper and lithium, metals critical for batteries and technology. Fraud allegations and logistical failures undermine democratic credibility and raise concerns about whether election results truly reflect the people's will. This matters globally because Peru's stability affects regional trade, US relations with Latin America, and the broader health of democracy in the Western Hemisphere, reminding us that elections worldwide shape international relationships and economic partnerships.

tech-ai.title: Why trust is a big question at the Elon Musk-OpenAI trial
tech-ai.summary: Elon Musk, the billionaire founder of Tesla and SpaceX, is suing OpenAI, the AI company behind ChatGPT, over whether the company kept its original promise to develop AI for humanity's benefit rather than just making money. Sam Altman, OpenAI's CEO, is at the center of the case with questions about whether he can be trusted. This trial matters because it reveals how AI companies are supposed to work and whether their leaders are being honest with the public. The case explores whether OpenAI broke promises it made when the company started, potentially affecting how future AI companies operate and how much we can trust them with powerful technology.

tech-ai.title: University of Arizona students boo Eric Schmidt's AI cheerleading during commencement
tech-ai.summary: Eric Schmidt, the former CEO of Google, gave a graduation speech at the University of Arizona where he enthusiastically talked about artificial intelligence as a positive force. However, students repeatedly booed him when he mentioned AI, showing real anger and anxiety about the technology's future. This reaction matters because it demonstrates that young people entering the job market are genuinely worried that AI will eliminate jobs and make it harder for them to find work. Schmidt acknowledged these concerns exist, but his optimistic speech clashed with graduates' real fears about climate change, job security, and machines replacing human workers in the coming years.

tech-ai.title: Revamped Siri will reportedly offer auto-deleting chats
tech-ai.summary: Apple is redesigning its Siri voice assistant to work more like ChatGPT while making privacy (keeping your personal information secret) its main selling point. The new Siri, coming in iOS 27, will let users automatically delete their chat history after 30 days, one year, or keep it forever—giving you control over your data. This matters because Apple is trying to stand out from competitors like Google and Microsoft by promising that your conversations won't be stored forever by the company. In a world where tech companies often collect and keep everything you say, Apple is betting that teenagers and their parents will choose iPhones specifically because they care about not having their private chats saved indefinitely.

tech-ai.title: Apple's Siri revamp could include auto-deleting chats
tech-ai.summary: Apple is overhauling Siri, its voice assistant, with a major focus on privacy—the idea that your personal information stays yours and isn't shared with others. The upcoming version will likely feature auto-deleting chats, meaning conversations automatically disappear instead of being permanently stored by Apple's servers. This development is significant because most AI chatbots like ChatGPT save everything you write, which raises concerns about whether your secrets and personal thoughts could be hacked or misused. By offering auto-delete options, Apple is trying to convince users that choosing their devices means your AI conversations won't haunt you forever, making privacy the main reason to pick Apple over other tech companies competing in the AI space.

tech-ai.title: Chatbots at the drive-thru are just the beginning
tech-ai.summary: McDonald's started using AI chatbots to take drive-thru orders back in 2021, beginning with just 10 locations in Chicago before expanding further. These voice-ordering systems use artificial intelligence to understand what customers want and process their requests without a human employee taking the order. This matters because it shows how AI is quietly replacing human jobs in everyday places where you'd never expect it, from fast-food restaurants to potentially many other service industries. Understanding this trend helps teenagers see that AI isn't just something in science fiction—it's already making real changes to the job market and how businesses operate, affecting which types of jobs will be available when you're old enough to work.

tech-ai.title: ElliQ is a surprisingly helpful companion robot for older adults
tech-ai.summary: ElliQ is a small robot designed to keep older adults company and encourage them to stay active, socialize, and pursue hobbies—all things that help manage serious diseases like Parkinson's. A caregiver tested ElliQ with their mother and found it genuinely helpful in ways they didn't expect, motivating her to exercise and engage with life again when her medication stopped working as well. This matters because as the world's population ages and fewer young people are available to care for seniors, AI robots could fill a crucial gap in helping elderly people maintain their health and mental well-being. For teenagers, this shows that AI isn't just about replacing jobs—it can also solve real human problems, especially for people who are isolated or struggling with their health.

tech-ai.title: Anthropic's $1.5B copyright settlement is getting messy as judge delays approval
tech-ai.summary: Anthropic, an AI company that created Claude (a ChatGPT competitor), agreed to pay $1.5 billion to settle a lawsuit with authors and publishers who claim the company used their books to train AI without permission. However, a judge delayed approving the deal because lawyers are accused of rushing to finalize it so they can collect $320 million in fees (payment for their work). This controversy matters because it raises important questions about whether AI companies should have to pay for using people's creative work, and whether legal settlements actually protect creators fairly. For teenagers who might become writers, artists, or creators, this case determines whether AI companies will have to respect your work and pay you when they use it to train their systems.

tech-ai.title: TechCrunch Mobility: The AI skills arms race is coming for automotive
tech-ai.summary: As self-driving cars and electric vehicles become more common, car companies are entering an 'arms race'—intense competition where everyone tries to have better technology than their rivals—to develop AI skills and talent. Traditional automakers like Ford and General Motors are competing with Tesla and new tech companies to hire the best AI engineers and programmers who can make smarter vehicles. This matters because the future of cars depends on AI: autonomous vehicles (cars that drive themselves) need brilliant AI to avoid accidents and make split-second decisions. For teenagers, this means the automotive industry is completely transforming, creating new career opportunities in AI and engineering, while also making older car-related jobs potentially obsolete as technology takes over driving and maintenance tasks.

tech-ai.title: Microsoft is retiring Teams' Together Mode
tech-ai.summary: Microsoft is shutting down a feature called 'Together Mode' in Teams, the video-meeting software many schools and offices use for remote work. Together Mode used AI to cut out your background and place your head and shoulders into a virtual conference room with other meeting participants, creating the illusion you were all sitting together in person. The company is retiring it because it felt gimmicky and unnecessary, and they want to simplify Teams by removing features people don't really use or need. This matters because it shows how AI experiments don't always succeed—even major tech companies have to admit when a feature isn't actually helpful, and it teaches us to think critically about whether new technology actually improves our lives or just feels cool for a moment.

tech-ai.title: If you're giving a commencement speech in 2026, maybe don't mention AI
tech-ai.summary: Commencement speakers (people who give graduation speeches) are finding it difficult to excite graduating students about their future when that future seems to revolve around artificial intelligence taking over jobs and creating uncertainty. Students about to enter the real world aren't thrilled about speeches celebrating AI when they're worried the technology will replace them in the job market. This trend matters because it reveals a generational divide: while tech leaders and older generations often see AI as exciting progress, young people entering adulthood feel anxious and skeptical. The article highlights that graduating seniors have genuine concerns about whether AI will create or destroy job opportunities, making it hard for older generations to pitch an optimistic future built on technology that feels threatening to your economic survival.

finance-markets.title: Oil Rises as Trump Renews Threats Against Iran With Hormuz Shut
finance-markets.summary: President Donald Trump is pressuring Iran to end weeks of military conflict and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway through which roughly 30% of the world's oil passes. As tensions escalate, oil prices have risen for three consecutive days. The Strait of Hormuz is essentially a chokepoint—if it stays closed, countries can't get the fuel they need, which drives up prices globally and affects everything from gas at the pump to heating costs. This matters to you because higher oil prices mean higher prices for flights, road trips, and anything shipped by truck or plane, potentially hitting your family's wallet.

finance-markets.title: Bond Traders See Shift Toward New Era of Higher Yields
finance-markets.summary: Bond traders believe the world is entering a period of permanently higher borrowing costs, with 30-year government bond yields climbing toward 5%—the highest level in 20 years. Bonds are essentially IOUs that governments and companies issue to borrow money; when yields (interest rates) rise, it becomes more expensive to borrow. This shift is driven by war-related inflation fears sparked by the Iran conflict disrupting oil supplies. Higher borrowing costs ripple through the economy: mortgages get pricier, credit card rates climb, and companies struggle to afford new projects, potentially slowing job growth and making big purchases like homes or cars less affordable for young adults entering the workforce.

finance-markets.title: Global Bond Rout Deepens, Stocks Fall as Oil Gains: Markets Wrap
finance-markets.summary: Global financial markets are experiencing a "rout"—a sharp, widespread selloff—as the Iran-Hormuz deadlock pushes oil prices higher and causes bonds to lose value. Stocks, which are pieces of ownership in companies, are also falling from recent record highs as investors grow nervous about economic instability. When bond markets tank and stock markets decline simultaneously, it signals broader economic anxiety: investors fear that high inflation and war disruptions will damage corporate profits and reduce people's purchasing power. For teenagers, this matters because stock market volatility can affect job availability, college savings plans in your family's 529 accounts, and overall economic growth that determines whether employers are hiring or laying off workers.

finance-markets.title: The looming energy crunch: Supply squeeze due to the Iran war will get worse before it gets better
finance-markets.summary: The ongoing Iran conflict is creating an energy supply squeeze—meaning there won't be enough oil and fuel to meet global demand in the coming weeks and months. The Strait of Hormuz, controlled by Iran, is essentially the world's oil highway; when it's blocked by conflict, energy prices spike worldwide and businesses struggle to operate normally. This supply crunch will likely worsen before improving because military tensions show no signs of easing. For you, an energy crunch translates to higher prices at gas pumps, increased electricity bills at home, and costlier flights and deliveries—basically everything powered by oil or electricity becomes more expensive. It can also slow economic growth, making it harder for companies to hire new workers.

finance-markets.title: Gulf freight rates jump as shipping companies turn to trucks to move cargo
finance-markets.summary: Shipping companies in the Gulf region are abandoning sea routes due to the Iran conflict and instead using trucks to transport goods, causing freight rates—the cost to move cargo—to skyrocket. A truck can carry only a fraction of what a ship can hold, making it far more expensive and inefficient to move large quantities of goods by road. Businesses are now facing thousands of dollars in extra costs per shipment. These increased shipping expenses get passed down to consumers through higher product prices on everything imported or shipped long distances. Since many everyday items—electronics, clothing, furniture—travel by sea before reaching stores, this shipping crisis means you'll likely see price increases on goods you buy, directly impacting your household's shopping budget.

finance-markets.title: Gold Extends Slump as Hormuz Quagmire Keeps Inflation Fear High
finance-markets.summary: Gold prices are declining as ongoing uncertainty about the Strait of Hormuz reopening keeps inflation fears elevated. Inflation occurs when the general price level of goods and services rises, eroding your money's purchasing power—essentially, your dollar buys less stuff. Gold is traditionally viewed as a "safe haven" investment that holds value during economic turmoil, but paradoxically, inflation fears are actually pushing gold down as investors sell it to raise cash and adjust their portfolios. The Hormuz situation creates a double bind: war-driven oil shortages trigger inflation concerns, which makes financial markets unstable, causing investors to reduce all risky holdings including gold. This matters because persistent inflation fears can lead central banks like the Federal Reserve to keep interest rates high, making savings accounts pay better but also making borrowing for cars, homes, or education more expensive.

finance-markets.title: Yardeni Urges Fed to Drop Easing Bias or Lose Control of Rates
finance-markets.summary: Ed Yardeni, a respected financial analyst, is warning the Federal Reserve (America's central bank, which controls interest rates) that it's falling behind the bond market and risks losing control of borrowing costs if it doesn't act fast. "Easing" means lowering interest rates to stimulate borrowing and spending; Yardeni is saying the Fed needs to abandon this strategy and raise rates to combat inflation. When the Fed loses control of rates, it means markets—not the government—are setting how expensive it is to borrow, which can lead to chaotic, unpredictable financial conditions. This matters because the Fed's interest rate decisions affect everything from student loan costs to job creation to whether your family can afford a mortgage. If the Fed loses credibility, financial instability could worsen, potentially triggering recessions that cost people jobs.

finance-markets.title: White House says Trump and Xi agreed on 'board of trade' at summit
finance-markets.summary: President Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping met and announced a new 'board of trade'—essentially a formal channel for discussing economic disputes between the US and China. This comes amid an ongoing economic standoff where both countries have imposed tariffs (taxes on imported goods) to pressure each other into negotiations. A board of trade is meant to reduce tensions by creating structured dialogue, potentially preventing a full-blown trade war that could raise prices on everything Americans import from China. For teenagers, this matters enormously: a US-China trade war drives up prices on phones, laptops, clothing, and toys—most of which are manufactured in China. The fact that both leaders are talking is a positive sign that cooler heads might prevail, which could help keep prices stable and preserve jobs in trade-dependent industries.

finance-markets.title: Australia Orders Chinese Investors to Sell Rare Earth Stakes
finance-markets.summary: Australia's government has ordered Chinese investors to sell their ownership stakes in Northern Minerals Ltd., a company that mines rare earth elements—special metals crucial for smartphones, electric vehicles, and military equipment. This is the second such intervention in two years, as Australia seeks to protect the company from Chinese control on "national security" grounds. Rare earth elements are so valuable and strategically important that countries view control over them as a security issue; China currently dominates global rare earth production, so Australia is trying to maintain domestic control. This matters because competition for rare earths is reshaping global supply chains and geopolitics. If Australia succeeds in keeping mining independent, it could diversify the world's rare earth supply, but it also signals rising tensions between Western nations and China over strategic resources essential for modern technology.

finance-markets.title: Billionaire Gina Rinehart Bets $100 Million on US Defense Stocks
finance-markets.summary: Gina Rinehart, Australia's richest person, invested almost $100 million in US weapons manufacturers and mining companies during the first quarter of 2026. Rinehart's investment signals confidence that defense spending will remain high due to ongoing global conflicts, particularly the Iran war and broader geopolitical tensions. When billionaires make massive bets on defense stocks, it reflects their belief that governments will continue spending heavily on weapons, military technology, and related industries. This matters because defense industry growth can create jobs in manufacturing, engineering, and research, but it also suggests the world expects prolonged military conflict rather than peace. Additionally, when billionaires influence which industries grow, it shapes the economy's direction and can affect which careers will be in demand for your generation—defense tech might boom while other sectors lag.

science-space.title: Scientists reverse Alzheimer's in mice with breakthrough nanotechnology
science-space.summary: Researchers have achieved a major breakthrough in treating Alzheimer's disease using tiny engineered particles called nanoparticles. Scientists at leading research institutions created these microscopic machines to target toxic proteins in the brain that destroy memory and thinking abilities. The nanoparticles work by activating the brain's natural cleanup system, which normally removes waste but fails in Alzheimer's patients. In experiments, elderly mice treated with this therapy showed dramatically improved memory and behaved like healthy younger mice. This is significant because Alzheimer's affects millions of people worldwide, stealing memories and independence. While this research is still in early stages with mice, it offers real hope that human treatments could follow within years, potentially preventing or reversing cognitive decline in people.

science-space.title: Scientists reversed memory loss by recharging the brain's tiny engines
science-space.summary: Researchers discovered that mitochondria—the tiny energy-producing structures inside every cell—might be the root cause of memory loss in diseases like Alzheimer's and dementia. Think of mitochondria as the batteries powering your brain cells; when they malfunction, brain cells starve for energy and stop working properly. Scientists created a new tool that temporarily boosts mitochondrial power in the brain and successfully restored memory in mice with dementia-like symptoms. This finding is revolutionary because it points to an entirely new way to treat memory loss: instead of just attacking disease symptoms, doctors could recharge brain cells' energy systems. This research could lead to breakthrough treatments for millions suffering from dementia and other neurodegenerative diseases in the coming years.

science-space.title: NASA Draws on Industry for Mars Telecommunications Network
science-space.summary: NASA has launched an official request asking private companies to help build a communications network for Mars—essentially a high-tech infrastructure for talking to robots and astronauts on the Red Planet. Right now, sending messages to Mars rovers takes 20+ minutes because of the massive distance, and data from missions moves slowly. NASA wants to create powerful orbital satellites around Mars that can transmit high-definition video, scientific data, and critical mission information at much faster speeds. This partnership between government space agencies and private companies (like SpaceX and Blue Origin) represents how space exploration is evolving. The network matters because future human missions to Mars will need reliable communication for safety and science success. Without fast, dependable internet on Mars, astronauts couldn't conduct complex experiments or handle emergencies effectively.

science-space.title: Giant radio telescope sees Artemis 2 astronauts on Orion flying around the moon
science-space.summary: A massive radio telescope in West Virginia successfully tracked NASA's Artemis 2 spacecraft as it carried four astronauts around the moon, demonstrating incredible precision in deep space navigation. The Green Bank Telescope, one of the world's largest steerable radio dishes, captured signals from the tiny Orion spacecraft traveling nearly 240,000 miles away—so distant that the astronauts appeared as just four pixels on the telescope's instruments. This achievement proves scientists can track human spacecraft in lunar orbit with extraordinary accuracy, which is essential for future moon missions and eventual Mars expeditions. Artemis 2, which launched in May 2026 with NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman and Victor Glover plus Christina Koch and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen, represents humanity's return to lunar exploration after 50+ years. This successful tracking demonstrates the technology infrastructure is ready for longer human space missions.

science-space.title: NASA, Industry Prepare Cryogenic Fuel Technology Demo
science-space.summary: NASA is partnering with Eta Space, a private Florida company, to test a game-changing technology called LOXSAT—essentially building a gas station in space. The system manages cryogenic fluids (super-cold liquid oxygen and hydrogen) that can be stored and transferred between spacecraft orbiting Earth or the moon. Right now, rockets must carry all their fuel from Earth, which limits how far they can travel; imagine a car that can't refuel. With in-space fuel depots, spacecraft could refuel in orbit, dramatically extending their range for deep space missions to the moon, Mars, and beyond. This technology demonstration will happen aboard an orbiting spacecraft, testing whether these ultra-cold fuels can be safely managed in weightlessness. Success here could revolutionize space exploration by making long-term missions to distant planets economically practical for the first time.

science-space.title: Stunning 150-million-year-old stegosaur skull rewrites dinosaur evolution
science-space.summary: Paleontologists in Spain have discovered the most complete stegosaur skull ever found in Europe—a 150-million-year-old fossil belonging to Dacentrurus armatus, the iconic plated dinosaur with distinctive bony plates running down its back. This discovery is extraordinary because stegosaur skulls are extremely fragile and almost never survive fossilization intact, making this find incredibly rare and scientifically valuable. The well-preserved skull allows scientists to study the brain structure, teeth, and jaw mechanics of these ancient creatures in unprecedented detail, revealing new information about how stegosaurs evolved and hunted. Studying fossils like this helps scientists understand how dinosaurs adapted to their environments and developed the strange features that made them successful for millions of years. This discovery reminds us that new secrets about Earth's prehistoric past are constantly waiting to be uncovered, and each fossil fills in missing pieces of evolution's story.

science-space.title: Watch NASA's new Mars helicopter rotor break the speed of sound
science-space.summary: NASA is developing a next-generation fleet of helicopters designed to fly through Mars's incredibly thin atmosphere, and engineers are testing rotors so advanced they must spin faster than the speed of sound to generate enough lift. Mars's atmosphere is only about one percent as dense as Earth's, making flight extremely difficult—it's like trying to fly a helicopter in the upper reaches of Earth's atmosphere where air is extremely thin. NASA's new rotor blades spin at revolutionary speeds, creating the pressure differences needed to push the aircraft upward despite the thin Martian air. These Mars helicopters will eventually scout terrain for rovers and astronauts, providing aerial reconnaissance that ground vehicles cannot accomplish. This technology represents a major engineering feat that will fundamentally change how humans explore other planets. When successful, these aircraft will open new possibilities for scientific discovery and human safety on future Mars missions.

science-space.title: The real reason exercise makes you stronger isn't what you think
science-space.summary: Scientists have discovered that exercise works more like brain training than previously understood—your brain cells remain highly active long after your workout ends, and these lingering signals actually teach your body to build endurance and strength. Most people assume muscles do all the work during exercise, but new research shows your brain plays an equally critical role in adapting to physical training. Researchers used mice in experiments where they blocked specific brain cells while the animals exercised; even though the mice ran normally, they failed to build endurance, proving the brain's involvement is essential. This discovery explains why consistent training gradually makes you stronger and faster—your brain is essentially rewiring itself to support better performance. Understanding this brain-body connection could revolutionize fitness science and recovery strategies. For teenagers, this means the mental aspect of training—staying focused and motivated—might be just as important as the physical effort you put in.

science-space.title: SpaceX Dragon capsule delivers science and supplies to space station
science-space.summary: A SpaceX Dragon cargo spacecraft successfully delivered 6,500 pounds of scientific experiments and supplies to the International Space Station, demonstrating the reliability of commercial space resupply missions that have become routine in modern space exploration. SpaceX, the private rocket company founded by Elon Musk, regularly launches Dragon capsules on cargo runs to the ISS, which orbits Earth carrying astronauts from multiple countries conducting cutting-edge research. These deliveries include fresh food, equipment, spare parts, and scientific instruments that keep the space station operational and enable crucial experiments impossible to conduct on Earth. The International Space Station exists in microgravity—a near-weightless environment that allows scientists to study how materials, biology, and physics behave differently than on Earth. These regular cargo missions are essential infrastructure for human space exploration, allowing the ISS to continue its mission of advancing scientific knowledge that benefits people on Earth.

science-space.title: Fresh Food Delivery for Space Station
science-space.summary: Northrop Grumman's Cygnus XL cargo spacecraft delivered a shipment of fresh produce—including oranges, apples, and onions—to astronauts aboard the International Space Station, where NASA astronauts Jack Hathaway, Jessica Meir, and Chris Williams, plus ESA astronaut Sophie Adenot, are conducting experiments in microgravity. Fresh food deliveries to the ISS are important for astronaut health and morale during their months-long missions orbiting Earth, though the environment's microgravity creates challenges for eating and food storage. The Cygnus spacecraft, built by Northrop Grumman, is one of several commercial vehicles that resupply the space station with necessities from cargo to experiments to oxygen. These regular supply missions are crucial infrastructure that keeps the ISS operational and enables the continuous human presence in space. The ability to feed and care for astronauts in orbit represents humanity's expanding capability to live and work in space for extended periods, paving the way for longer missions to the moon and Mars.

energy-climate.title: Neoenergia plans $10bn investment in Brazil by 2030
energy-climate.summary: Neoenergia, a Brazilian subsidiary of Iberdrola, has renewed three electricity distribution concessions in Brazil, announcing plans to invest nearly 50bn reais ($10.2bn) by 2030.

energy-climate.title: What is ‘Premium Content’ on Energy Monitor?
energy-climate.summary: Energy Monitor has introduced new ‘Premium Content’ for its readers – here’s an explanation of what’s changing and what isn’t.

energy-climate.title: TWP submits consent application for Bowdun wind farm in Scotland
energy-climate.summary: TWP submitted an offshore consent application to the Scottish Government’s Marine Directorate for development of Bowdun Offshore Wind Farm.

energy-climate.title: Hydrogen’s next phase will favour execution over scale
energy-climate.summary: Hydrogen's market outlook points to a sector with renewable power access and durable frameworks increasingly deciding which projects proceed.

school-education.title: OPINION: Putting students on school boards treats young people as participants in their own democracy, but only if adults listen to them
school-education.summary:
Public school is the first place most Americans meet democracy. It is also, for almost all of them, the last place they experience it without a vote. School boards are elected by adults, staffed by adults and run for adults. They make decisions every week about buildings full of young people who get no say. […]

asia-pacific.summary:
The Hong Kong Observatory received more than 10 reports of a locally felt tremor from a magnitude 5.2 earthquake that struck Guangxi early on Monday.
According to the forecaster, the epicentre was located about 550km (342 miles) west-northwest of Hong Kong, with a focal depth of about 10km.
“The Observatory received more than 10 locally felt reports of the tremor, which lasted a few seconds,” it said on Monday.

asia-pacific.summary:
A man in southern China suspected of poisoning an online celebrity dog which is famous for collecting plastic bottles in the street has been caught after its owner called the police.
The French Bulldog nicknamed Xiaobai won the hearts of millions last year for diligently collecting plastic bottles for its owner.
It has earned over 100,000 yuan (US$15,000) over the past five years as its owner, surnamed Zhang, sold the waste bottles.

asia-pacific.summary:

The deadlock over the Strait of Hormuz was one of the key issues discussed during the summit between Donald Trump and Xi Jinping last week.
As the impasse continues, here is where things stand.
Iran prepares new transit mechanism

asia-pacific.title: White House mass prayer event seeks to reclaim US Christian roots
asia-pacific.summary:
US President Donald Trump and top cabinet officials addressed thousands of Americans at a mass prayer rally in Washington on Sunday – an event critics saw as an overt display of Christian nationalism undermining the separation of church and state.
The gathering was organised by the White House as part of a programme of celebrations for America’s 250th anniversary and billed as an opportunity to revive the idea of a country founded on Christian principles.

asia-pacific.summary:
Israeli strikes killed seven people in Lebanon on Sunday, including an Islamic Jihad commander, Lebanese authorities and state media said, despite a fragile ceasefire as Hezbollah called US-brokered talks between the two countries a “dead end”.
